title: Five minutes of doing nothing
subtitle: A short guided practice for deliberately allowing stillness without a task to complete.
excerpt: A five-minute meditation from Day 19 of the SLEEP program. The practice is simple: sit or lie down, do nothing, and let everything that arises pass without acting on it. The doing nothing is the practice.
sourceNotes: Original to homemade.education. Drawing on the non-doing (wu wei) tradition in Taoist philosophy and the do-nothing meditation approach found in various contemplative practices. Framing adapted from Day 19 of SLEEP: A 30-Day Tapping Intensive (Rebecca J Page, 2025), "Stillness without dread."

practice.whenToUse: Use any time you feel the compulsion to be productive when rest is what's needed — or when sitting still without a task feels uncomfortable.

content.text: A five-minute practice from the Day 19 theme. The instruction is the whole thing: do nothing. No technique, no focus, no goal to reach. Five minutes where nothing is required of you.
Sit in a chair or lie down — wherever you can be still without needing to do anything. Set a timer for five minutes. Put the phone face-down.

The only instruction is this: do nothing. Don't try to breathe in a particular way. Don't try to clear the mind. Don't try to relax. Don't try to do the practice 'well'. Just be in the room for five minutes with nothing to accomplish.
Thoughts will arrive. Let them. You don't need to follow them or push them away. They can be there.

The urge to do something will probably arrive too — check the phone, remember a task, decide what's for dinner. Notice the urge. Don't act on it. The timer will tell you when the five minutes are done.
If 'doing nothing' feels unbearably uncomfortable, that discomfort is exactly why this practice is worth doing. Sit with it for one more minute before deciding to stop.
When the five minutes are up, take one breath before moving. Notice whether anything has shifted — in the body, in the pace of the mind, in the felt sense of the room. Then carry on with the day.

Original to homemade.education. The non-doing approach draws on the wu wei tradition in Taoist philosophy and appears in various contemplative practices across cultures. The framing here is adapted from the Day 19 theme of SLEEP: A 30-Day Tapping Intensive (Rebecca J Page, 2025).
